title: Implement secure secret management for Kubernetes (Sealed Secrets, SOPS, or ESO)
description: Replace the current approach of storing secrets as base64-encoded values in Helm values files with a secure secret management solution. Integrate one of the following: (a) Sealed Secrets (encrypt secrets with the cluster public key and commit SealedSecret CRs to Git), (b) SOPS with age/KMS encryption of values files before commit, or (c) External Secrets Operator (ESO) pointing to AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, or HashiCorp Vault. Update the CI/CD pipeline to validate that no raw secret values are present in committed files. Document and automate the secure injection of secrets at deploy time.
subtasks: Integrate Sealed Secrets, SOPS, or ESO into the Helm chart and deployment workflow
Update all secret references in Helm templates to use the chosen solution
Remove committed example secrets containing real or placeholder values
Add a CI/CD step that scans for raw secrets in committed files and fails if found
Document the secret rotation and injection process for operators
Run a test deployment to verify secrets are decrypted and injected correctly
Run a security scan (e.g., TruffleHog, git-secrets) on the repo to ensure no secrets are present
acceptance_criteria: No raw secret values are present in any committed files
All secrets are managed via Sealed Secrets, SOPS, or ESO and injected securely at deploy time
CI/CD pipeline fails if a raw secret is detected in a commit
All services start successfully with secrets injected from the chosen solution
Documentation exists for secret rotation and injection
A test deployment using the new secret management approach completes successfully

title: Redesign JWT authentication architecture with dedicated identity provider and RS256 key distribution
description: Replace the current shared JWT_SECRET approach with a secure authentication architecture. Deploy a lightweight identity provider (e.g., Keycloak, Zitadel, or Auth.js) or designate one service as the token issuer. Switch all services to RS256 JWT signing, where only the issuer holds the private key and all other services use the public key for verification. Implement a JWKS endpoint for public key distribution. Update all JWT middleware to validate tokens using the RS256 public key. Ensure Morgan's tool calls use service-account JWTs with short TTLs.
subtasks: Deploy and configure an identity provider or designate a token issuer service
Generate RS256 key pair and configure issuer to sign JWTs with the private key
Expose a JWKS endpoint for public key distribution
Update all service JWT middleware to validate RS256 tokens using the JWKS endpoint
Update Morgan and all backend services to use service-account JWTs with short TTLs for internal calls
Document the new authentication flow and key rotation process
Run integration tests to verify cross-service authentication and token validation
Run a security scan (e.g., OWASP ZAP) against all endpoints to verify authentication enforcement
acceptance_criteria: All services validate JWTs using RS256 and the JWKS endpoint
No service stores or uses a shared symmetric JWT secret
Morgan and backend services use service-account JWTs with short TTLs
A compromised service cannot forge tokens for other services
Documentation exists for key rotation and authentication flow
Integration tests pass for all authentication scenarios
OWASP ZAP scan shows no authentication bypass vulnerabilities

title: Implement two-phase GDPR delete flow with API-layer confirmation and audit logging
description: Replace the current LLM prompt-based GDPR delete confirmation with a technical control at the API layer. Implement a two-phase delete flow: (1) POST /internal/gdpr/delete/:id returns a short-lived (5-minute TTL) confirmation token stored in Valkey, (2) DELETE /internal/gdpr/delete/:id?confirm_token={token} performs the actual deletion. Ensure the confirmation token is issued by Morgan's backend, not the LLM. Log both the confirmation issuance and the actual deletion in an audit log.
subtasks: Implement POST /internal/gdpr/delete/:id to issue a confirmation token and store it in Valkey with a 5-minute TTL
Implement DELETE /internal/gdpr/delete/:id?confirm_token={token} to validate the token and perform deletion
Ensure only Morgan's backend can issue confirmation tokens
Log both confirmation issuance and deletion events to a tamper-evident audit log
Update Morgan's GDPR tool to require two distinct API calls for deletion
Write integration tests for the two-phase delete flow, including token expiry and invalid token scenarios
Run an end-to-end GDPR delete test and verify audit log entries
acceptance_criteria: GDPR delete requires two distinct API calls with a confirmation token
Confirmation tokens expire after 5 minutes and cannot be reused
Only Morgan's backend can issue confirmation tokens
All GDPR deletions are logged with both confirmation and deletion events
Integration tests pass for all delete scenarios, including token expiry and invalid tokens
End-to-end test shows audit log entries for both confirmation and deletion

title: Enforce secure container image practices: pin image tags, add vulnerability scanning and image signing
description: Update all container image references to use pinned tags and digests (not :latest). Integrate container image vulnerability scanning (e.g., Trivy, Grype, or Snyk) into the CI pipeline for all images, including signal-cli, cloudflared, postgres, valkey, and custom service images. Block merges on CRITICAL severity findings. Add image signing with cosign to verify provenance.
subtasks: Update all Kubernetes manifests to use pinned image tags and digests for all containers
Integrate Trivy (or equivalent) into the CI pipeline to scan all images on PR and nightly
Configure the CI pipeline to block merges on CRITICAL severity findings
Implement image signing with cosign for all built images
Document the image update and scanning process
Run a full scan of all images and remediate any CRITICAL findings
Run a test deployment with only signed images
acceptance_criteria: No container image uses the :latest tag in any manifest
CI pipeline scans all images and blocks on CRITICAL findings
All images are signed with cosign and verified at deploy time
Documentation exists for image update, scanning, and signing
A test deployment with only signed images completes successfully

title: Harden GDPR internal endpoints with mTLS or Kubernetes ServiceAccount JWT authentication
description: Replace the X-Internal-Request header check on GDPR endpoints with cryptographic authentication. Implement mTLS using Cilium's identity-aware transparent encryption or require Kubernetes projected ServiceAccount JWTs. Each service calling GDPR endpoints must present its ServiceAccount JWT in the Authorization header; the endpoint validates the JWT and checks the sub claim matches the expected service identity.
subtasks: Implement mTLS or ServiceAccount JWT validation on all GDPR internal endpoints
Update all calling services to include their ServiceAccount JWT in the Authorization header
Validate the JWT and ensure the sub claim matches the expected service identity
Remove reliance on the X-Internal-Request header for access control
Write integration tests to verify only authorized services can access GDPR endpoints
Run a security scan (e.g., OWASP ZAP) to verify endpoint authentication
acceptance_criteria: All GDPR internal endpoints require mTLS or ServiceAccount JWT authentication
Only authorized services can access GDPR endpoints
X-Internal-Request header is no longer used for access control
Integration tests pass for all authorized and unauthorized access scenarios
OWASP ZAP scan shows no authentication bypass vulnerabilities

title: Enforce dependency pinning and lockfile validation across all stacks
description: Ensure all dependencies are pinned and lockfiles are committed and validated for Rust, Go, and Node.js stacks. Add CI checks that fail if lockfiles differ from what the package manager would produce. Integrate cargo deny and govulncheck for Rust and Go, and enforce frozen lockfile installs for Node.js. Consider enabling Dependabot or Renovate for automated security PRs.
subtasks: Require Cargo.lock, go.sum, and package-lock.json/bun.lockb to be committed and validated in CI
Add CI checks that fail if lockfiles are out of sync with the dependency manifests
Integrate cargo deny and govulncheck into the CI pipeline
Enforce frozen lockfile installs for Node.js (bun install --frozen-lockfile)
Enable Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency updates and security PRs
Document the dependency update and validation process
Run a full dependency audit and remediate any HIGH/CRITICAL findings
acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline fails if lockfiles are missing or out of sync
cargo deny and govulncheck run on every PR and block on HIGH/CRITICAL findings
Node.js installs use frozen lockfiles in CI
Dependabot or Renovate is enabled and configured
Documentation exists for dependency update and validation
A full dependency audit shows no unaddressed HIGH/CRITICAL vulnerabilities

title: Enable Valkey authentication and update all service connection strings
description: Configure Valkey (Redis) authentication for production by generating a random 32-byte password and storing it as a Kubernetes secret. Update the Opstree Redis CR to require authentication. Update the VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL in the sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap to include the password. Update all services to use the new connection string.
subtasks: Generate a random 32-byte password and store it as a Kubernetes secret for Valkey
Configure the Opstree Redis CR with requirepass or equivalent
Update VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL in sigma1-infra-endpoints to include the password
Update all service deployments to use the new connection string
Test all services for successful Valkey authentication
Write integration tests to verify unauthorized access is denied
acceptance_criteria: Valkey requires authentication in production
All services connect using the new authenticated connection string
Unauthorized access to Valkey is denied
Integration tests pass for both authorized and unauthorized access

title: Restrict Signal-CLI JSON-RPC endpoint to Morgan pods and require authentication
description: Restrict access to the Signal-CLI JSON-RPC endpoint (port 7583) by adding a CiliumNetworkPolicy that only allows ingress from pods with label app: morgan. If supported, configure signal-cli with a JSON-RPC password. Add this as an explicit subtask in the production hardening task.
subtasks: Create a CiliumNetworkPolicy in the signal namespace allowing ingress to signal-cli-svc:7583 only from pods with label app: morgan
Configure signal-cli with a JSON-RPC password if supported
Update Morgan's configuration to use the password
Test that only Morgan pods can access the endpoint
Run a network scan to verify no other pods can reach the service
acceptance_criteria: Only Morgan pods can access the Signal-CLI JSON-RPC endpoint
Signal-CLI requires a password for JSON-RPC access (if supported)
Morgan can send and receive Signal messages successfully
Network scan confirms no unauthorized access

title: Enforce Stripe webhook raw body validation before deserialization
description: Update the Stripe webhook handler in the finance service to ensure the raw request body is captured before any deserialization. Perform HMAC-SHA256 verification against the raw bytes. Only after successful verification should the body be deserialized as JSON. Add an explicit integration test for tampered body/signature scenarios.
subtasks: Refactor the Stripe webhook handler to capture raw body bytes before deserialization
Perform HMAC-SHA256 verification using the Stripe webhook secret
Deserialize the body only after successful verification
Add integration tests for tampered body and invalid signature scenarios
Run a security scan (e.g., OWASP ZAP) against the webhook endpoint
acceptance_criteria: Webhook handler validates signature before deserialization
Tampered body or invalid signature returns 400 and does not process the event
Integration tests pass for all validation scenarios
OWASP ZAP scan shows no deserialization or signature validation vulnerabilities

title: Harden file upload endpoint with magic byte validation, size limits, and path sanitization
description: Update the social-engine file upload endpoint to validate file types using magic byte inspection, enforce maximum file size limits per file and per request, and sanitize event_id before using it in S3/R2 key paths. Add explicit test cases for these checks.
subtasks: Implement magic byte validation for JPEG/PNG files using the infer crate or equivalent
Enforce maximum file size (e.g., 20MB per file, 100MB total per request)
Validate and sanitize event_id as a UUID before using in key paths
Reject uploads with invalid file types, oversized files, or invalid event_id
Add integration tests for all validation scenarios
acceptance_criteria: Only valid JPEG/PNG files are accepted based on magic bytes
Oversized files or requests are rejected
event_id is validated as a UUID and sanitized
Integration tests pass for all validation scenarios

title: Restrict Morgan GDPR admin skill to authenticated operator channels
description: Update Morgan's configuration to restrict the GDPR admin skill to only be activatable via an authenticated operator channel (e.g., a specific Signal number or web admin panel with its own JWT scope). Disable the admin skill for the public web chat widget. Add a channels restriction field to the admin.yaml skill manifest and explicit tests to verify enforcement.
subtasks: Add a channels restriction field to the admin.yaml skill manifest
Configure Morgan to only enable the admin skill for authenticated operator channels
Disable the admin skill for the public web chat widget
Add integration tests to verify the GDPR delete tool cannot be triggered via the web chat channel
Document the channel restriction configuration
acceptance_criteria: GDPR admin skill is only accessible via authenticated operator channels
Public web chat cannot trigger GDPR delete operations
Integration tests pass for all channel restriction scenarios
Documentation exists for channel restriction configuration

title: Centralize GDPR deletion audit logging across all services
description: Define a centralized audit.gdpr_deletions table accessible to all services. Standardize the record schema and ensure the GDPR orchestration tool writes a single aggregated audit record after all fan-out deletions complete. Update individual services to also write their own records. Add a migration to create this table in the shared audit schema.
subtasks: Create a centralized audit.gdpr_deletions table with the standardized schema
Update all services to write GDPR deletion records to the centralized table
Update the GDPR orchestration tool to write an aggregated audit record after fan-out
Add database migrations to create the table and grant access to all services
Write integration tests to verify audit records are created for all deletions
acceptance_criteria: All GDPR deletions are logged in the centralized audit.gdpr_deletions table
The table is accessible to all services
Integration tests pass for audit logging in all deletion scenarios

title: Enforce Kubernetes Pod Security Admission and container securityContext standards
description: Update all Deployment manifests to include a securityContext with runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 1000, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, and capabilities: { drop: ['ALL'] }. Apply Pod Security Admission labels to all namespaces enforcing restricted policies. Add a CI check using kubesec or kube-score to validate manifests.
subtasks: Update all Deployment manifests to include the required securityContext fields
Apply pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted labels to all namespaces
Integrate kubesec or kube-score into the CI pipeline to validate manifests
Write integration tests to verify pods run with the correct security context
Document the pod security standards and enforcement process
acceptance_criteria: All pods run with the required securityContext settings
All namespaces enforce restricted Pod Security Admission
CI pipeline fails if manifests do not meet security standards
Integration tests pass for pod security context
Documentation exists for pod security standards

title: Enforce TLS for all database connections
description: Update all database connection strings to enforce TLS (sslmode=require or sslmode=verify-full). Configure CNPG to issue TLS certificates and mount the CA cert into service pods. Update all service connection pool configurations to require TLS.
subtasks: Update CNPG_SIGMA1_URL to include sslmode=require or sslmode=verify-full
Configure CNPG to issue TLS certificates and mount the CA cert into service pods
Update all service connection pool configurations to require TLS
Test all services for successful TLS connections to the database
Write integration tests to verify connections fail without TLS
acceptance_criteria: All database connections enforce TLS
Services fail to connect if TLS is not enabled
Integration tests pass for TLS enforcement
CA certificates are mounted and used by all services

title: Implement security event logging and anomaly alerting
description: Add security-specific alerting rules and logging. Configure Prometheus alerts for authentication failures and rate limit violations. Create a dedicated Loki log stream for GDPR operations with anomaly alerts. Write Morgan tool call audit logs to a tamper-evident append-only log.
subtasks: Add Prometheus alert for >10 auth failures per minute per service
Add Prometheus alert for >50 rate limit violations per minute per service
Create a dedicated Loki log stream for GDPR operations
Configure alerting for more than 3 GDPR deletions in 24 hours
Write Morgan tool call audit logs to a tamper-evident append-only log
Test all alerting rules and log streams
Document the security event logging and alerting configuration
acceptance_criteria: Prometheus alerts fire on authentication and rate limit anomalies
Loki log stream records all GDPR operations
Morgan tool calls are logged in a tamper-evident manner
Documentation exists for all alerting and logging rules

title: Define and enforce Content Security Policy and HTTP security headers for website and mobile app
description: Define a strict Content Security Policy (CSP) in next.config.ts and as Cloudflare headers. Add X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, and Referrer-Policy headers. Ensure the Morgan tunnel domain is included in script-src and connect-src. Apply the same headers to the mobile app webview if applicable.
subtasks: Define a strict CSP in next.config.ts and wrangler.toml headers
Add X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, and Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin
Include the Morgan tunnel domain in script-src and connect-src
Test the website and mobile app for CSP violations using browser dev tools and security scanners
Document the CSP and security header configuration
acceptance_criteria: CSP and security headers are present on all website and mobile app responses
Morgan chat widget loads successfully under the CSP
No CSP violations are reported in browser dev tools or security scans
Documentation exists for CSP and header configuration
